metadata.summary: Controller-Service-Repository pattern for Node.js backends. Controllers validate input and map errors; services own business logic; repositories encapsulate ORM/SQL.

content.title: Node.js Service Layer
content.summary: Controller-Service-Repository pattern for Node.js/TypeScript backends. Controllers validate input with Zod and map errors to HTTP; services own all business logic and throw typed domain errors; repositories encapsulate ORM/SQL. Each layer has a single responsibility and no cross-layer imports (controller never imports repository).

why.p: Thin controllers and pure services are unit-testable without an HTTP server. Repository abstraction lets the service layer swap DB implementations. Domain errors — not HTTP codes — inside services mean the same logic can be invoked from HTTP, gRPC, queues, or CLIs. eslint-plugin-boundaries + dependency-cruiser enforce the layering automatically.

list.item: Express, Fastify, NestJS, or Hono services with non-trivial domain logic.
Multi-entry backends (HTTP + gRPC + queue consumers) reusing the same service.
Projects targeting high unit-test coverage — controller-heavy code resists testing.
LLM-driven implementation: three specific shapes (controller/service/repository) reduce free decisions and bugs.
Toy CRUD apps where every handler is db.find().lean() — extra layers add ceremony with no payoff.
Edge functions / serverless with strict cold-start budgets — DI containers slow boot.
Pure proxies / BFFs with no domain logic — route → fetch → respond is enough.
Realtime-heavy apps (WebSocket gaming, MQTT) where event handlers don't fit request-response shape.

rule.statement: Controllers do exactly four things: validate input (Zod/TypeBox), call one service method, map errors via central ErrorMapper, return DTO. Any logic beyond this belongs in the service.
rule.rationale: Thin controllers are stateless and trivially testable; fat controllers resist unit testing because they require an HTTP context.

rule.statement: Services accept and return domain types; they never import Express/Fastify Request or Response, and they throw typed domain errors — not HTTP status codes.
rule.rationale: Framework-agnostic services can be called from HTTP handlers, gRPC, queue consumers, and CLIs without modification.

rule.statement: Repositories expose intention-revealing methods (findActiveByUserId) over generic find(filter); they own all ORM/SQL logic and never leak query builders to callers.
rule.rationale: Generic find(filter) enables callers to compose unindexed filters at runtime, causing silent full-table scans.

rule.statement: Dependencies must only flow downward: Controller → Service → Repository. Cross-layer imports (controller → repository, service → framework) are a hard violation caught by eslint-plugin-boundaries.
rule.rationale: Upward or cross-layer imports break testability and couple business logic to transport details.

example.title: Direct repository import in controller
Service delegation from controller

section.name: dependency-injection

rule.statement: Wire dependencies through constructor injection; never use module-level singletons or static service locators in files that need to be unit-tested.
rule.rationale: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and replaceable with mocks in unit tests.

example.title: Manual DI container setup

section.reference: UserService with DI, domain errors, business logic

section.name: domain-error-classes

rule.statement: Define a typed AppError hierarchy (NotFoundError, ConflictError, UnauthorizedError, ValidationError); services throw domain errors, never raw Error or HTTP status codes.
rule.rationale: Typed domain errors give the central ErrorMapper precise mapping targets; raw errors produce generic 500s and lose observability.

example.title: AppError hierarchy

section.reference: Full AppError hierarchy + central ErrorMapper middleware

rule.statement: Translate domain errors to HTTP responses in a single Express errorHandler middleware; never translate domain errors inside individual controllers.
rule.rationale: Per-handler translation diverges over time; a central mapper ensures consistent status codes, response shapes, and logging across all endpoints.

example.title: Express error handler middleware

antipattern.description: Business logic (hashing passwords, checking duplicates, sending emails) placed directly in Express route handlers.
antipattern.reason: Route handlers cannot be unit-tested without an HTTP server; logic cannot be reused from queue consumers or CLIs.

antipattern.description: Service methods importing and calling Prisma/Sequelize/Knex directly instead of through a repository interface.
antipattern.reason: Direct ORM usage in services couples business logic to the DB layer, preventing mock-based unit tests and storage swaps.

antipattern.description: Generic repository method findAll(filter) where callers compose unindexed filter predicates.
antipattern.reason: Runtime-composed filters produce full-table scans that only appear under production load; index coverage cannot be verified statically.

antipattern.description: Per-controller try/catch that wraps all errors in res.status(500).json(), swallowing domain error context.
antipattern.reason: Swallowing domain errors loses error code, stack trace, and structured detail; the central ErrorMapper must handle translation.

antipattern.description: Zod schema updated without regenerating the inferred TypeScript type (or vice versa), causing runtime/type divergence.
antipattern.reason: Schema and type out of sync means the compiler approves code that fails at runtime validation.

antipattern.description: Service class registered as singleton in the DI container while carrying request-scoped state (auth context, tenant ID).
antipattern.reason: Singleton services with mutable request-scoped fields leak state across concurrent requests.

section.name: boundary-enforcement

rule.statement: Run dependency-cruiser and eslint-plugin-boundaries in CI to block controller→repository and service→framework imports automatically.
rule.rationale: Human review alone misses import violations in large PRs; automated tools enforce the contract on every change.

section.reference: CI script using dependency-cruiser to block cross-layer imports

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A four-stage flow per feature. (1) Spec → DTOs: an agent generates Zod / TypeBox schemas for request, response, and domain entities; controllers and services derive types from the schemas. (2) Service skeleton: agent creates <feature>.service.ts with constructor injection of repository + clients, returns domain types only. (3) Controller: thin handler validates with the schema, calls the service, maps domain errors to HTTP via a central error mapper. (4) Tests: unit tests for the service with mocked repo, integration tests for the controller using supertest or light-my-request (Fastify). faion-sdd-executor-agent enforces "controller has no business logic; service has no req/res" as a structural rule.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — gates: controllers don't import repos, services don't import Request/Response, every public service method has unit tests with ≥1 happy + ≥1 error path.

A layer-linter subagent (worth creating): walks the dependency graph and fails when controller→repo or service→framework imports appear. Cheap to run with dependency-cruiser / eslint-plugin-boundaries.
A DTO-from-schema subagent: given Zod / TypeBox schemas, generates request/response types and OpenAPI fragments.
feature-executor (skill) — sequence schema → service → repo → controller → tests as ordered SDD tasks.
password-scrubber-agent — scrub fixture data and example logs before publishing.
Controllers do four things: validate input (Zod/TypeBox), call one service method, map errors via a central mapper, return DTO. Anything else is a smell.
Services own business logic, accept and return domain types, throw domain errors. They never know about Request, Response, or HTTP status codes.
Repositories own SQL / ORM specifics. Expose intention-revealing methods (findActiveByUserId) over generic find(filter) to keep performance in your hands.
Use a DI container (awilix, tsyringe, NestJS) once you exceed ~10 services; manual wiring becomes a tax.
Validate at the edges: Zod / TypeBox at controllers, infer types throughout. No any, no as unknown as T.
Log with pino and a request-scoped child logger (req.log); propagate trace IDs into services so logs join up.
Use a central ErrorMapper translating domain errors to HTTP. Every new domain error gets a registered mapping or a CI lint failure.
Transactions: thread an unitOfWork (Drizzle / Prisma $transaction callback) through service methods that span repos.
Test pyramids: heavy on service unit tests with mocked repos, plus integration tests using a real DB (Testcontainers) for repos and route-level tests for controllers.
Don't reach for NestJS unless you'll use modules, guards, interceptors, and pipes; otherwise its DI and decorators are pure overhead.
Agents move HTTP concerns into services (res.status(404).json(...)) because that's the dominant pattern in the training set. Reject and require domain errors.
LLMs invent generic findAll(filter) repository methods, encouraging callers to compose unindexed filters at runtime. Force intention-revealing names.
Agents skip dependency injection and import repositories directly inside services, defeating testability. The boundary check above catches this.
DTO drift: agents update Zod schemas without regenerating types or vice versa. Use z.infer<typeof schema> and lint for divergence.
as any / @ts-ignore proliferation. Add eslint-rule-ban-ts-comment and no-explicit-any and treat them as errors.
Async error swallowing: agents wrap service calls in try { ... } catch { res.status(500) } and lose stack + context. Centralize via the error mapper, never per-handler.
Agents over-mock in service tests, ending up testing the mocks. Require ≥1 integration test per public service method using a real DB.
DI container misuse: agents register everything as singleton; request-scoped state (auth, tenant) leaks across requests. Pin lifetimes in the container.
Agents introduce cyclic deps when a service imports another service that imports the first. madge --circular in CI prevents.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: any change to the central error mapper or the DI container registration must be human-approved — both have system-wide impact.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Anaemic services: every method is repo.findById-and-return; the layer adds cost without value. A symptom, not the pattern's fault.
Manual DI bowls of spaghetti: hand-wired constructors get unmanageable past ~30 services; tools like tsyringe, awilix, or NestJS DI become necessary.
Repository over-abstraction: hiding ORM features (eager loading, query builders, transactions) behind generic find(filter) cripples performance and forces leaky workarounds.
Cross-service transactions: two services calling two repos can't rely on a request-scoped DB transaction unless explicitly threaded; bugs manifest as partial writes.
TypeScript boundaries: agents widen DTOs to any to "make it compile"; runtime payloads diverge from types.
Error-mapping leaks: domain errors end up as 500s because controllers don't translate them to HTTP — kills observability.
Test fixtures explode: every service test mocks 3 repos; brittle and slow. Symptom of poor seam design.
"Service" becomes "the place I dump utility functions" — naming rot.

template.purpose: UserService with DI, domain errors, business logic
Thin Express controller — validate, call service, map errors
Bash script using dependency-cruiser to block cross-layer imports in CI
